_metadata.source: American Board of Internal Medicine - Laboratory Tests Reference Ranges
_metadata.purpose: Evidence-based evaluation for automated health diagnostics
_metadata.disclaimer: Reference ranges are for educational purposes. Clinical interpretation requires healthcare professional consultation.

Hemoglobin.clinical_significance: Oxygen-carrying capacity of blood. Low values indicate anemia; high values may indicate polycythemia.

Hematocrit.clinical_significance: Percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells. Related to hemoglobin levels.

WBC.clinical_significance: Indicates immune system function. Elevated in infections/inflammation; low in immunosuppression.

Platelet Count.clinical_significance: Essential for blood clotting. Low values increase bleeding risk; high values may increase clotting risk.

RBC.clinical_significance: Number of red blood cells. Related to oxygen transport capacity.

Glucose.clinical_significance: Blood sugar level. Elevated values indicate diabetes risk or diabetes.

HbA1c.clinical_significance: Average blood glucose over 2-3 months. Primary diabetes diagnostic and monitoring marker.

Cholesterol Total.clinical_significance: Total cholesterol level. Elevated values increase cardiovascular disease risk.

LDL.clinical_significance: Bad cholesterol. Primary target for cardiovascular risk reduction.

HDL.clinical_significance: Good cholesterol. Higher values are protective against cardiovascular disease.

Triglycerides.clinical_significance: Type of fat in blood. Elevated levels increase cardiovascular disease risk.

Creatinine.clinical_significance: Kidney function marker. Elevated values indicate impaired kidney function.

BUN.clinical_significance: Kidney function and protein metabolism marker. Elevated in kidney disease or dehydration.

ALT.clinical_significance: Liver enzyme. Elevated in liver damage or disease.

AST.clinical_significance: Liver and heart enzyme. Elevated in liver damage, heart attack, or muscle injury.

Bilirubin Total.clinical_significance: Liver function and red blood cell breakdown marker. Elevated in liver disease or hemolysis.

Alkaline Phosphatase.clinical_significance: Liver and bone enzyme. Elevated in liver disease, bone disorders, or bile duct obstruction.

TSH.clinical_significance: Thyroid function regulator. Elevated in hypothyroidism; low in hyperthyroidism.

Free T4.clinical_significance: Free thyroid hormone levels. Indicates thyroid function status.

FSH.clinical_significance: Reproductive hormone. Regulates ovarian and testicular function.

LH.clinical_significance: Reproductive hormone. Triggers ovulation and testosterone production.

Prolactin.clinical_significance: Hormone regulating lactation and reproductive function. Elevated levels may indicate pituitary disorders.

Testosterone Total.clinical_significance: Primary male sex hormone. Important for reproductive and metabolic function in both sexes.

Vitamin D.clinical_significance: Bone health, immune function. Low levels associated with bone disease and various health conditions.

Calcium.clinical_significance: Bone health, nerve and muscle function. Imbalances affect multiple body systems.

Sodium.clinical_significance: Electrolyte balance, fluid regulation. Abnormal levels can be life-threatening.

Potassium.clinical_significance: Heart rhythm, muscle function. Severe abnormalities can cause cardiac arrest.

Iron.clinical_significance: Essential for hemoglobin production. Low levels cause anemia; high levels may indicate overload.

Ferritin.clinical_significance: Iron storage marker. Better indicator of iron deficiency than serum iron alone.

Uric Acid.clinical_significance: Purine metabolism marker. Elevated in gout and kidney disease.

CRP.clinical_significance: Inflammation marker. Elevated in infections, inflammatory diseases, tissue damage.

ESR.clinical_significance: Non-specific inflammation marker. Elevated in many inflammatory and infectious conditions.
